title: Organisms & Life Processes
objective: Students will explore organisms & life processes concepts including How animals adapt to environments, Variation & Survival Advantage, Inheritance Vocabulary and more.

steps.content: Today we're going to learn about How animals adapt to environments. Get ready for an adventure!
Identify how animals and plants are adapted to suit their environment and understand that adaptation may lead to evolution over time
Time to try this out: Define adaptation as a feature that helps an organism survive in its environment
Can you explain how a camel's humps, a cactus's thick skin, or a polar bear's white fur help them survive where they live, and how these adaptations developed over time?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about How animals adapt to environments. Define adaptation as a feature that helps an organism survive in its environment Give at least three examples of adaptations in different organisms and explain how each helps survival
Today we're going to learn about Variation & Survival Advantage. Get ready for an adventure!
Use evidence to explain how variations in characteristics among individuals of the same species may provide advantages in surviving, finding mates, and reproducing
Let's practice together! Explain that variation within a species means some individuals have traits better suited to their environment
Can you explain why a faster rabbit in a group might survive longer because it can escape predators better than slower ones?

Great job! Today we learned about Variation & Survival Advantage. Explain that variation within a species means some individuals have traits better suited to their environment Give examples of advantageous variations (e.g. camouflage, speed, drought resistance in plants)
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Inheritance Vocabulary!

Use vocabulary for variation and inheritance — inherited characteristic, acquired characteristic, variation, offspring, trait, species, breed, genetic, environment — and apply these when comparing organisms and explaining similarities and differences within and between species
Time to try this out: Distinguish between inherited and acquired characteristics with a correct example of each
Can you explain why children look similar to but not exactly like their parents — using words like 'inherited' or 'variation'?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Inheritance Vocabulary. Distinguish between inherited and acquired characteristics with a correct example of each Use 'variation' correctly to describe differences within a species and explain what causes variation
Have you ever thought about Inherited characteristics? Let's find out together!
Analyse and interpret data to provide evidence that plants and animals have traits inherited from parents and that variation of these traits exists in a group of similar organisms
Time to try this out: Define inherited traits as characteristics passed from parents to offspring
If you measures the height of all the sunflowers in the garden, can they see that they're all sunflowers but each one is a slightly different height?

Great job! Today we learned about Inherited characteristics. Define inherited traits as characteristics passed from parents to offspring Collect or interpret data showing variation within a group (e.g. different heights, colours, sizes)
Let's discover the amazing world of The Digestive System!
Describe the simple functions of the basic parts of the human digestive system: mouth, oesophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine
Let me show you something cool. Name the main organs in order: mouth, oesophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine
Can you trace the journey of a sandwich through the body — from chewing in the mouth all the way to the intestines?

You did it! Here's what we explored about The Digestive System: Name the main organs in order: mouth, oesophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine Describe the function of each (mouth chews, stomach breaks down, small intestine absorbs nutrients)
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Types of Teeth!
Identify the different types of human teeth (incisors, canines, molars) and describe their functions in eating
Let me show you something cool. Name three types of teeth: incisors, canines, molars
Can you point to different teeth in their mouth and explain that front teeth cut food while back teeth grind it?
Great job! Today we learned about Types of Teeth. Name three types of teeth: incisors, canines, molars Describe the function of each: incisors cut, canines tear, molars grind
Have you ever thought about Traits: inherited and environmental? Let's find out together!
Use evidence to support the explanation that traits can be influenced by the environment as well as inheritance
Let me show you something cool. Explain that traits result from both inheritance and environmental factors
Can you explain why two identical seeds might grow into different-sized plants if one gets more sunlight than the other?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Traits: inherited and environmental: Explain that traits result from both inheritance and environmental factors Give examples of environmental influence on traits (e.g. sunlight affects plant growth, diet affects animal size)
